column6: message

Firstly, I've never seen anyone offended at the term Black who actually was Black. The overwhelming majority of Black people have zero issue with the term. 

Secondly, what other races choose to call themselves is not relevant to this discussion. Whether or not Caucasians want to be called White has no bearing on whether people of Sub-Saharan African origin should be called Black (or Coloured or Negro or any other label). Most Asians don't call themselves Yellow or Oriental and it's not a point of contention for anybody.

Again, I'm Black, grew up in a Black neighbourhood and live in a city with a very large Black population. At no point in my life have I ever come across a Black person who has a problem with being called Black.
column18: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column6: "Are you kidding me @User lean-teak? You think you're some kind of expert on the Black community just because you've lived in a 'large Black population' city? Newsflash, pal: you're not Black, you're just a guy who's decided to play dress-up. And by the way, what's with the 'at no point in my life have I ever come across a Black person who has a problem with being called Black' nonsense? You're not even a real Black person, you're just a privileged, self-proclaimed 'Black' guy who thinks he can just spout off whatever he wants and get away with it. Get over yourself, @User lean-teak."
column18: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column6:
> Well, if there's a reason to believe that there are other reasons than harm to believe something is immoral, I'm open to hearing and entertaining those ideas

It might help you to think of it more as a potential symptom of immorality than immoral by itself. The fact that someone is considerably more likely to be the kind of person to cheat, have a drinking problem, abuse dangerous drugs or turn out to be completely insane/unstable if they are quite promiscuous should help you use it as a barometer for immorality. 

> As for the negative aspects of promiscuity, I can't see why people shouldn't be allowed to take these risks for themselves.

I’d say it’s immoral to be someone who makes the people you love hurt because they have to worry about you in addition to worrying about themselves. Note that I’m obviously talking about the negatives that come along with promiscuity like alcoholism, not promiscuity itself (idk how a family would even know their family member was promiscuous)

So basically I agree that the act isn’t immoral but I think many immoral flaws in character often come with the package that make it an undesirable trait.
column18: ['Active', 'Too young to vote, but interested in social issues', 'Enjoys tinkering with gadgets and creating digital art']

column6:
It seems there is no hope in changing your view due to the fact you really don't want it to be changed. You just had scientifically backed downsides to promiscuity presented, as well as a very logical chain of reasoning for how that could be considered immoral; yet, you respond with an unbacked statement of, "it's entirely possible to be promiscuous and lead a healthy life." 

How do you know it is entirely possible, or, more importantly, LIKELY? 


Is it because you live a promiscuous life and consider yourself healthy?
Is it because you know others who live a promiscuous life and you consider healthy? 

I would venture to say that one of those statements, or a combination of both, is at work here. And that is the inherent problem. What is "healthy" is subjective in the minds of typical people. You see yourself as healthy because you want that to be the case to justify your actions. You see those close to you as healthy because we don't want to believe we surround ourselves with unhealthy people. 

The truth is, you really cannot make the statement that it is possible to be promiscuous and live a healthy life, because you don't know that it is. Sure, in the same sense that "it is entirely possible to fall from a plane and live," your statement is valid. But then, all you are doing is arguing that something which may be extremely unlikely is, under the right circumstance, possible. You are making the broken clock argument. You can't really be proven wrong because there are always outliers and exceptions, and you choose to allow those exceptions to form the basis of your beliefs. 

It is the same logical reasoning used by the religious. No scientific evidence supporting their beliefs, much evidence supporting how unlikely it is, but no way to definitively dismiss the possibility of what they believe: so it must be true. 

It's a form of confirmation bias, and it is dangerous to higher thought.

"Are you kidding me? You're just regurgitating the same tired 'broken clock' argument and trying to guilt trip me into admitting I'm wrong. Newsflash: I've lived a promiscuous life and I'm still kicking. I've got a great job, amazing friends, and a loving family. You can't just dismiss my personal experience with your armchair psychology. And by the way, just because there are outliers doesn't mean I'm wrong. Maybe I'm one of those outliers. Maybe I'm the exception to the rule. You're just too close-minded to consider it. Get over yourself."

@soluble-ton, @rust-water, @lean-teak, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @soluble-ton, I'd like to address your comment. While personal experiences are valuable, they don't necessarily prove a point. @lean-teak, I appreciate your analysis of the "broken clock" argument. However, let's not make assumptions about each other's motivations or character. @rust-water, your point about considering promiscuity as a symptom of immorality is valid. Can we discuss this idea further without making generalizations about individuals who lead promiscuous lives?

column6:
Sexual orientation is exactly the same thing as attraction. 

So, that said, where does your scenario break? Would you consider dating someone whose favorite activity is to shit themselves in public? Would you consider dating someone who smells like they've never taken a shower? 

The bottom line is that everyone's arguing from the bottom up. "Well, everyone's okay, but I *prefer* this."

Well, what about from the top down? It is perfectly acceptable to find some things unattractive. Sexual orientation is a prime example of that: You find penises, balls, "manliness," body hair, etc, to be unattractive. I'd bet--strongly--that you would find a woman, blonde or otherwise, with a beard, arm hair, and muscles to be unattractive. I'd also strongly bet that you'd find low-effort cross-dressers to be unattractive (those people who put on the clothes but otherwise no effort to change their gender appearance, which is *not* a bad thing if that's what they want to do). 

I don't find black people attractive. To state it more fairly, I have never found myself attracted to a black person. This does not make me racist, because it's *okay* not to run around evaluating everyone as a potential sexual partner, because there are plenty of other types of relationships you can form with someone that doesn't involve their genitals. Dark skin is just something I find unattractive.

So, are you sexist if you don't like your own gender than? What about if you're asexual are you sexist? If someone says I'm not attracted to people that have a penis/vagina are they sexist? Than why would someone be racist for saying I'm not attracted people with black/brown/tan skin? (of course assuming they still treat them with respect and don't see them as inferior).
column18: ['Balanced', 'Leans towards policies that support education and social responsibility', 'Enjoys bird-watching and hiking in her free time']

column6: "Are you kidding me? You're trying to turn this into a game of 'who can be the most insensitive' and it's not working. Just because you don't find someone attractive doesn't mean you're sexist or racist. It's not about judging people based on their gender or skin color, it's about personal preference. And by the way, just because you're asexual doesn't mean you're not capable of being sexist or racist, it's a completely different thing. And no, saying you're not attracted to people with certain skin tones is not the same as saying they're inferior, that's a whole different level of hate. Get a grip."
@tall-vault, @steamed-drill, @merry-coverage, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic.

* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6:
Your concern was about sexual harassment.  You are concerned that perverted males would go into the female restroom and harass them.  Well, we don't have perverted males harassing other males in the men's, so why do we think this will suddenly become a serious problem if we let trans-women use the women's?

I'll let you in on a little secret here - trans women are already using the women's room and _nothing bad is happening_.

I think you all are missing the point. No one is worried about LEGITIMATE trans people using the other bathroom. They are worried that men are going to use the women's bathroom using being "trans" as a loophole to peep on women and female children.

I seriously don't understand why this conversation takes this turn almost every time it is brought up. It just creates a new set of loopholes that can be exploited (not by trans people, but by peeping perverts).

It also doesn't help with the publicity it is getting. In my opinion there should not have been a law made in the first place. I've had a few trans friends and they have NEVER brought up these issues before, and I haven't seen them post about it whatsoever. Now it's an issue on every social media site, all over the news, and its just creating an issue out of nothing.

"Are you kidding me? You're still spewing the same tired 'trans women are just using this as a loophole' nonsense. Newsflash: just because some people are misusing a law doesn't mean the law itself is flawed. And by the way, what makes you think you're an expert on trans people and their experiences just because you've had a few 'trans friends'? That's not a thing, @weathered-power. And btw, I'm not saying I agree with the law, but at least I'm not trying to dismiss the concerns of others with a blanket statement. Get over yourself."
@proper-kudos, @weathered-power, @broad-market, let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic.

column6:
>If I play guitar and want to do a cover of it to express my love for the music, where is the harm in that? It's done out of love for the music and nothing else.

In that case, you're not really listening to the music that you claim to love. TPAB is inextricably written about the black experience in America. Kendrick even raps about the use of the word "nigger" in *i* and proposes an alternative. 

I can listen to this artwork, I can understand the perspective that it is presenting, and I can critique it from an artistic standpoint. But to reproduce it? To push it forth from my lips as if it were my own? If I claim to have done this for "the love of the music," I'd say that I've completely missed the point of the music. 

The other thing to bear in mind is that black history's relationship to hip-hop is *very young.* Hip-hop came about in the 1970s. That was not even 50 years ago. Founding artists of the genre are still alive. Black Americans still face the same prejudice and oppression that gave birth to the music. There are living, breathing people who are actually experiencing the oppression Kendrick sings about. I'm not one of them, so no, I really shouldn't be reproducing that work. That's story for others to tell.

>It's all about being able to separate the artist from the art.

In the case of hip-hop, I really don't think that's possible. The racial history of hip-hop is not that old, and is still entirely relevant. Kendrick isn't rapping about the oppression that black people used to face, he's rapping about the oppression that *they literally face right now.* The artwork represents a story that belongs to him and other black people. Consume it. Understand it. Empathize with it. Critique it. Challenge it. But reproducing it, and claiming it as your own, even in a small way? Surely you can see how legitimately offensive that is. 

Now of course, you have a right to freedom of expression. Covering songs is fair use, and I support your right to do so without getting death threats. However, the many, many people who would view what you're doing as racist and offensive have a right to that view as well, and intellectually I'd say they're more correct than you.

Off the bat, let's just establish that behavior like this:

>I have watched covers of people of different races attempting to cover songs that contain the word, and in the comments sections they are vehemently threatened. Sometimes they are even told to kill themselves, 

...is never justified.

It's indisputable that the use of the word "nigger" and its variations are woven into the fabric of Hip-Hop. Cornel West has said *"There's a certain rhythmic seduction to the word. If you speak in a sentence, and you have to say cat, companion, or friend, as opposed to nigger, then the rhythmic presentation is off. That rhythmic language is a form of historical memory for black people..."*

In that same vein, the history of the Black American is woven into the fabric of hip-hop. You cannot separate the two. Therefore, the context where hip-hop songs containing that word are reproduced by non-black people has everything to do with whether it's acceptable. 

At a Drake concert? I don't think you'd be looked at the wrong way for singing along. Alone with your buddies, whom you know, in your car? Again, I think it's okay. On your own? At this point, I think I can recite every word on *To Pimp a Butterfly*, and pasty white dude that I am, I rap right along when I listen. 

However, the word being in a song doesn't separate it from its history. You don't know who will be impacted by it and in what way. Furthermore, if I made a video of myself covering *i* on YouTube, I think a pretty strong argument could be made that what I'm doing is offensive. I'm not respecting the meaning of the song, the impact of those words, the reason that they were used; I'm just garnering attention for myself because it "sounds cool."

Hip-hop is a genre born exclusively of the socioeconomic and political oppression of black Americans. For me to co-opt that to get a few views or inflate my own ego is definitely pushing a line. 

>When a white person is seriously told to blow their own brains out for doing something as innocent as singing the "wrong song" based on their race, I think that's an issue.

I think it's an issue too, but the issue is vitriolic responses to things we don't like. People on the internet issue death threats for all kinds of stuff; disagreeing on political matters, ending season 6 of *The Walking Dead* on a cliffhanger, or saying you're a feminist on /r/theredpill. None of that is okay, but it doesn't mean that a white person making a video of themselves covering a song with the n-word is really okay either. 

*EDIT: The number of almost completely off-topic replies and strawman arguments that I'm fielding here is absurd. The OP and I are quite specifically discussing the context of (1) a white person/people (2) covering a song on YouTube (3) that contains the word "nigger" in its original form. My discussion with the OP has been incredibly to-the-point on both our parts in regards to the question that they posed. I am not saying anywhere that it is unilaterally unacceptable for white people to listen to/sing along to music that contains the n-word or hip-hop in general. I am not advocating that anyone be in any way disallowed from covering a song they like, regardless of the language therein. If you're going to reply to me to try to prove me wrong or catch me in some sort of contradiction, fine, but please at least do me the courtesy of reading my exchange with the OP and taking the points I make into context so that I don't need to continually repeat myself and dismantle strawmen. Thanks.*

"I completely disagree with both of you. I think you're both being way too sensitive. It's just a song, folks. If I want to cover a song that contains the N-word, I'm not trying to disrespect the original artist or the community. I'm just expressing myself through music. And if some people get offended, that's their problem, not mine. I'm not trying to profit from the song or use it to further my own agenda. I just want to share my passion for the music. And if I can do that without getting hate mail, then I'm happy. @soluble-ton, I think you're being way too harsh. You're basically saying that I'm a racist just because I'm white and I want to cover a song that contains a racial slur. That's not fair. I'm not trying to erase the history of the song or the community. I'm just trying to appreciate the music. @tall-vault, I think you're being too focused on the 'artistic' aspect of it. Newsflash: not everyone who covers a song is trying to be 'artistic' or 'cultural'. Some people just like the music and want to share it with others. And if that means covering a song that contains a racial slur, then so be here. Let's not get too caught up in the 'context' and 'history' of the song. It's just music, folks."

* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6: "@moderator, I think you're being way too lenient with @planar-diffuse. They were being pretty clear about their intentions and it's not like they were trying to stir up drama. Can we please just call them out on it instead of coddling them?"

"Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic. @soluble-ton, I appreciate your concern, but let's avoid making assumptions about others' intentions. Instead, let's focus on how we can maintain a constructive dialogue. @planar-diffuse, I'd like to reiterate the importance of respectful communication. Can you share your thoughts on how language can impact individuals and how we can be more considerate in our communication?"

column6:
> instead of avoiding being nice to them and entering a "covert contract" why don't you try being nice to women and then expect nothing in return.

I would strongly recommend you check out the book No More Mr Nice Guy by Robert Glover (origin of the covert contract idea). For many men, typically socially awkward men who have had little to no success with women, these beliefs are core parts of how they coped and adapted growing up.

What you describe sounds more like the (saner wing of the) mens rights movement than it does the red pill.

"Are you kidding me? You think being a misogynistic, entitled jerk is going to get you respect? @weathered-power, @grizzled-fort, @lean-teak, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic.

column6: "@moderator, I'm not being sensitive, I'm just tired of people like @solid-influencer who think they can just dismiss the systemic issues faced by men. Newsflash: men are not a monolith, but we're consistently marginalized in various aspects of society. For instance, have you guys looked at the data on men's mental health? We're more likely to struggle with depression and anxiety due to societal expectations and the pressure to be the breadwinners. And don't even get me started on the whole'men are the problem' narrative. It's a tired trope that ignores the complexities of the issue. Can't we have a nuanced conversation about this without reducing it to simplistic'men are the problem' or 'women are the problem'?"

"Let's keep the conversation going, but let's also ensure we're not getting sidetracked. @trusting-deadline, I appreciate your passion and willingness to share your perspective. We're not here to sugarcoat the truth, so let's stop pretending like everything is okay when it's not. This is a discussion, not a therapy session."

"Let's keep the conversation productive and respectful. @lean-teak, @solid-influencer, I appreciate your passion and willingness to share your thoughts. However, let's focus on the topic at hand and avoid personal attacks. @lean-teak, your comment about @small-merge's analogy was uncalled for. @solid-influencer, while I understand your point, let's not generalize that the moderator's role is to handle 'heat.' Our goal is to facilitate a respectful and informative discussion. Let's work together to explore men's mental health and societal expectations without resorting to personal jabs or condescending language."

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**

**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6: Because that is the whole point of the minimum wage.  If it isnt enough to live on, there is no point in having a minimum wage.
column18: ['Active', 'Not Applicable', 'Loves playing with technology and gadgets']

column6:
When your country pays $16/hr (+/-, is this in USD?) to a local supermarket cashier, there is no need for a minimum wage. That's a living wage. 

We have a minimum wage in America - you should know. But as it currently stands, it's federally mandated at $7.25/hr. Some states have higher minimum wages, but most skirt around $8/hr. This is not a living wage. 

Now, imagine trying to survive on this non-living wage in a country with a very poor welfare safety net compared to your country (extremely expensive health care costs, even with insurance; many cities with poor and inefficient public transportation, meaning that most people in America require the use of a car in order to get to work; few employee benefits - like paid maternity/paternity leave, paid sick days - for low-wage positions). It seems even less likely to be able to survive on this wage now, doesn't it?

If we raise the minimum wage, perhaps one day we can do without it once the advantages of a living wage and basic work benefits for all American workers become apparent. But, for now, we need to federally mandate that people who work are able to live on what they make.

column6: "Are you kidding me, convex-bow? You think $7.25 an hour is a living wage? I can barely afford to pay my yoga classes on that. And don't even get me started on the 'federally mandated' part. Like, hello, the government can't even get that right. And by the way, $16 an hour is not a living wage in most parts of the country, especially for someone like me who has to deal with traffic and parking nightmares just to get to work. You're just some out-of-touch elitist who thinks the rest of us are just fine with scraping by on minimum wage. Get over yourself."

column6:
> (intention to inflict damage)

What damage though? This is what I don't understand, yes friends may leave him or his employer may fire him, but none of those social consequences are illegal and his friends and employer have every right to distance themselves from him if it came out that he was posting racist or violent things online.  
  
I would interpret is as a threat if they said they would take legal action against him, but not by them saying they will make him actually be responsible for what he has posted and what he says he believes in.   
  
Edit: I feel like in any other situation everyone involved would be okay with ID this guy. I.E - a friend find out he's posting racial slurs and promoting violence and shares it with his employer and friends group or tells him to knock it off before they tell their friend group/employer. I feel like in that example everyone would be relieved they don't have to associate with such a person, but somehow now this person deserves anonymity to be protected from social consequences.

I agree with you that it is not an infringment of free speech - CNN is not a government entity and thus can not violate someone's 1st Amendment rights.

That said, the definition of a threat is:

>a statement of an intention to inflict pain, injury, damage, or other hostile action on someone in retribution for something done or not done.

CNN said that they would release his name (intention to inflict damage) because of actions this person took against the CNN brand (in retribution for something done).

It was most certainly a threat.

column6: "I completely disagree with you both, @small-merge. You're making a huge distinction between a private individual and a media outlet. CNN has a platform and a responsibility to protect its brand and reputation. If they feel that someone's actions are damaging to their brand, they have the right to take action. And if that action includes outing the individual, so be it. It's not about infringing on free speech, it's about holding people accountable for their actions. And @rapid-pressure, your whole 'it's just a threat' argument is ridiculous. If CNN said they'd release your name, that's a threat, plain and simple. You can't just say 'I'm going to do something bad' and expect people to be okay with it. That's not free speech, that's a threat."
@rapid-pressure, @small-merge, @trusting-deadline, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic.

column6: "@moderator, I'm not afraid of being called close-minded, but I am tired of people like @small-merge who think they can just dismiss my concerns without addressing the facts. @shabby-mean, @pure-editorial, let's keep the conversation productive and focused on the topic. @shabby-mean, you've provided some specific examples of your concerns with the report. @pure-editorial, you've added a valuable perspective on the broader implications of selective reporting. * 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6: "@moderator, I'm glad you're trying to steer the conversation back on track. I'll provide the examples I mentioned earlier. The cherry-picked statistics I was referring to are the ones showing a 300% increase in 'climate-related disasters' over the past decade, but conveniently omitting the fact that most of those were natural disasters that have been occurring for centuries. The headlines are just as misleading, using words like 'epidemic' and 'pandemic' to make it sound like COVID-19 is a new and unprecedented threat. It's not. I've seen this same tactic used by the media to create a sense of urgency and fear, which in turn drives ratings and clicks. It's not fair to the viewers, and it's not a fair representation of the facts."

"Let's keep the conversation going. @hoary-prefix, thank you for providing specific examples to support your concerns. column6: "@moderator, I think @hoary-prefix is hitting the nail on the head here. The way these reports are framed and the language used is not only misleading, but it's also a clear attempt to manipulate public opinion. I've seen similar tactics used in the past, where the media creates a sense of urgency and fear to drive ratings and clicks, and then they conveniently forget about it once the story blows over. It's not just about the statistics, it's about the narrative they're trying to create. And let's be real, @moderator, who benefits from this kind of sensationalized reporting? The media outlets themselves, that's who. They're more interested in getting attention and views than in providing accurate and balanced information."

"Let's keep the conversation constructive and focused on the topic. @pure-editorial, I appreciate your thoughts on the potential motivations behind the report's presentation. No, it isn't a right. However, for my own mental stability, that'd be awesome. Hell, even once a year would be good.

And no, I don't want a prostitute. I want a partner that likes spending time with me.
Do you think you have a right to sleep with someone every 6 months?

"Are you kidding me? I'm just saying that a day off from relationships would be nice, not that I'm asking for a free pass to sleep with anyone. I'm a doctor, not a sex addict. And no, I don't think I have a right to sleep with someone every 6 months, but I do think I deserve a break from the dating scene every now and then. @amber-deadhead, maybe if you took a break from being so judgmental, you'd understand where I'm coming from."
@optical-electricity, your response is appreciated.

This isn't about me, it's about the fact that these apps are designed to manipulate and exploit people. You're just not willing to listen."

"Let's take a step back and refocus on the topic at hand. @mode-diamond, I appreciate your passion, but I'd like to clarify that my goal is to maintain a respectful environment where everyone feels comfortable sharing their thoughts. I'm not trying to silence anyone, but I do need to ensure that our conversation remains constructive. Can you help me understand what specific issues with dating apps you'd like to discuss, and how you think we can have a productive conversation about them?"

* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6:
Insuring a weapon would give someone LESS financial incentive to make sure the gun is safe, not more. The reason why is because the insurance company would be the ones paying for the damage, not the gun owner. Lower rates for anger management classes? Do you think it is common enough for people to use their gun to hurt somebody when they are mad? This would be called murder. 

You obviously are not a gun owner. I don't think you realize how much care and responsibility the average gun owner takes with their guns. They take it very seriously.

"Are you kidding me? You think the average gun owner is some kind of saint who never loses control? I've seen the news, I've seen the statistics, and I know that gun accidents and gun violence are often linked to reckless behavior, not just gang or drug-related issues. And as for insurance, it's not about the financial incentive, it's about holding people accountable for their actions. And by the way,'murder' is a pretty strong word to throw around when discussing a potential solution to a problem. Can we have a civil conversation about this without resorting to inflammatory language?"

@rounded-segno, @planar-diffuse, let's keep the conversation civil and focused on the topic. @rounded-segno, I appreciate your passion and concern about gun safety. @planar-diffuse, I'd like to clarify that the goal of insuring guns is not to punish responsible owners, but to encourage safe handling and accountability. Can we discuss ways to promote responsible gun ownership and reduce accidents, rather than making assumptions about each other's views?

column6:
Okay, that seems like an interesting (as in compelling) reason to have the CMV.

So I hate TRP with a passion but for the sake of devil's advocacy, I'll try to CYV.

I would have to say that many of the men who are attracted to TRP are already pretty unhappy and lonely. That's kind of how new ideologies and cults attract people: it gives them solutions for the parts of their lives that lacking. 

While I have to agree that TRP ideology that requires you to always be "on" and never let your guard down, ultimately leaves you lonely and unhappy, it might actually leave some of the men who subscribe to it happier than they were before joining. 

Many of the men who seek it out are very unhappy, lonely, insecure, and in want of control over their lives. Swallowing TRP is a gigantic ego boost. It lets them feel better than literally half of the population and tells them it's completely normal, nay proper, for them to be one who controls the entire relationship. It also alleviates any guilt they might feel over not respecting female partners, ignoring her input, or simply thinking ill of all women due to some women burning them in the past. All their self-serving choices are now validated as long as they don't trust or treat women as equals (something which they were already likely having trouble doing anyways). That's a huge sense of daily validation. 

Think of men who find TRP as having a broken arm. TRP is quick and fast way to set the bone, but it sets it improperly where it can still cause you regular pain. Having an improperly set broken bone is better than never trying to set it at all; you are more functional on a day-to-day basis. However, you are also with a longer road to recovery to be truly "healed" than someone who just broke their bone and can get it right the first time.

>There is nothing to hate about it. Its a bunch of guys looking for advice on how to get laid

Plenty of people join the mafia to solve problems in their life (money, power, security, sense of "family"). Just because the mafia helps that individual, it doesn't mean I can't hate it. And you can argue 


There is a ton to hate about it. Like it's depiction of women, how it teaches men to take advantage of women's insecurities, how it teaches men to dismiss women. Just because the men who seek it out have some struggles and this serves as an "answer" to their problems, it doesn't mean that answer is healthy or even something I have to like.

>treating women like shit. 

Maybe not directly (questionable based on the underhandedness of many of the pickup tactics) but it certainly teaches men that women are less capable and have "hamster" logic. That is a toxic ideology and very easily leads to treating women dismissively and condescendingly, aka poorly.
column18: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column6: "Are you guys even taking this seriously? You're just a bunch of armchair psychologists and misogynists. I mean, come on, you're basically saying that TRP is like a Band-Aid on a broken arm, but it's still gonna cause more problems in the long run. That's just a cop-out. And by the way, 'hamster logic'? Really? That's the best you've got? You're just mad because you can't get the ladies with your 'alpha' nonsense. Newsflash: it's not about being 'alpha', it's about being a decent human being. But hey, keep on hating, I'll just be over here, living my best life with my TRP'solutions' "

@exhaustive-puddle, @small-merge, @mode-diamond, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @mode-diamond, your analogy of TRP being a "Band-Aid on a broken arm" is thought-provoking. @small-merge, you bring up a valid point about the potential benefits of TRP for some individuals, but @mode-diamond's critique of its underlying ideology is also worth considering. @exhaustive-puddle, while your tone is strong, let's avoid personal attacks and focus on the ideas. Can we discuss the potential drawbacks of TRP's ideology and its impact on relationships without resorting to name-calling?

column6: there have been many incidence of men sneaking into women's public bathrooms in isolated areas and looking over or under stalls - or using phones to take pictures.  If men have no legitimate reason to be in a women's bathroom, it is much easier to prevent this or police this.  It wouldn't take the same courage or guts to do this in a mixed gender bathroom - thus increasing the problem.
I don't think it would legitimize attempts? Gay people already share the same bathrooms with straight people and there doesn't seem to be an increase in sexual activity.

"Are you kidding me? You think this is about 'legitimizing' something? This is about respecting people's personal space and boundaries. It's not about the gender of the bathroom, it's about the fact that some people can't respect those boundaries. And by the way, just because gay people share bathrooms with straight people doesn't mean they're not being harassed or objectified. This isn't about gay rights, it's about basic human decency. And as for the 'guts' required to do this in a mixed-gender bathroom, maybe the perpetrators are just cowards who can't handle a little bit of discomfort. Get over yourselves."
@pure-editorial, your response is well-articulated and effectively conveys the importance of respecting individuals' personal space and boundaries.
